original_abstract:
This review provides an overview of cosinor-based techniques, a cornerstone of rhythmometry used to analyze time series in chronobiology. Framed as a regression problem, the cosinor method offers a distinct advantage in its ability to handle non-equidistant data. It also allows for the derivation of confidence intervals for rhythmic parameters when periods are known—an approach that highlights the value of incorporating prior physiological information into the least squares procedure. 

While originally designed to extract insights from short or sparse datasets, the "extended cosinor" has evolved to support the analysis of long-term time series, facilitating both rhythm detection and precise parameter estimation. This paper evaluates the statistical assumptions necessary for effective cosinor application and describes diagnostic methods to ensure those assumptions are met. Furthermore, strategies for managing non-stationary data, such as gliding spectral windows and serial sections, are discussed. Through various practical examples, the application of these methods is shown to extend beyond simple circadian rhythms to the comprehensive mapping of complex biological time structures, known as chronomes.
humanized_abstract:
Review article: this article reviews cosinor-based methods, a family of algorithms commonly implemented in rhythmometry, the analysis of time series in chronobiology. These algorithms view the analysis as a regression problem. They have the advantage that they can be applied to non-equidistant sampling data. A by-product of the least squares formulation is that confidence intervals for the fitted components can be easily derived, provided the period of the rhythm is known. This method, as well as other features of the algorithms, embody the spirit of incorporating prior knowledge about the rhythms from physiological considerations into the least squares procedure.

We had originally developed the extended cosinor method for extracting rhythm information from short or sparse sequences of data; we now also use it for the detailed analysis of longer time series, i.e., for both rhythm detection and parameter estimation. We investigate the cosinor model statistical assumptions and the tools for the checks of these assumptions. Some of the possible procedures for treating non-stationarity of a biological signal including gliding spectral windows and serial sections are given. Illustrated with several examples, the results demonstrate that their use is not limited to the analysis of the circadian rhythm but can lead to a complete characterization of the biological time structures (chronomes) under investigation.

document.input:
This review provides an overview of cosinor-based techniques, a cornerstone of rhythmometry used to analyze time series in chronobiology. Framed as a regression problem, the cosinor method offers a distinct advantage in its ability to handle non-equidistant data. It also allows for the derivation of confidence intervals for rhythmic parameters when periods are known—an approach that highlights the value of incorporating prior physiological information into the least squares procedure. 

While originally designed to extract insights from short or sparse datasets, the "extended cosinor" has evolved to support the analysis of long-term time series, facilitating both rhythm detection and precise parameter estimation. Through various practical examples, the application of these methods is shown to extend beyond simple circadian rhythms to the comprehensive mapping of complex biological time structures, known as chronomes.
